worth_it_explanation: Samba TV's Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data comes from an opt-in sample of smart TVs, not a census, and the reported figures are modeled projections from that sample to the broader population. Campaigns with narrow geographies, niche networks, or small audience segments are working with thinner underlying samples, which means the projected numbers carry more uncertainty than the report may suggest. These checks confirm that the team understands the sample-based nature of the data, knows which cuts of the report are most and least reliable, and applies appropriate skepticism to small-base figures before using them to make budget or placement decisions.
intro: Samba TV measures TV exposure using ACR technology embedded in a footprint of opt-in smart TVs across certain brands and regions. ACR data is a sample, not a census: the devices in the network represent a subset of TV households, and Samba applies weighting and statistical modeling to project from that sample to the full population. The quality and reliability of any report cut depends on how many ACR devices fall into that particular segment. A national aggregate figure rests on a large sample base and is generally stable. A report sliced to a single designated market area (DMA), a specific cable network, or a narrow demographic may rest on a much smaller base, and the projected figure carries proportionally more uncertainty. These checks confirm that the campaign team understands what kind of data underlies Samba's reports, how to identify thin-sample cuts, and how Samba's projection methodology shapes the numbers before those numbers are used to guide budget or creative decisions.

audit_checks.title: Team does not understand that Samba TV reports are modeled projections from an opt-in ACR sample, not census counts
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Treating Samba TV reach or tune-in figures as census counts rather than modeled projections leads to overconfidence in the numbers, particularly for narrow cuts of the data. Decisions made on misread figures, including creative rotations, placement shifts, and budget reallocations, may be responding to noise rather than signal.

educate.base:
Samba TV's measurement is built on Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) technology. ACR works by capturing a fingerprint of what is displayed on a TV screen and matching it against a reference library of content and ads. The devices generating this data are opt-in smart TVs, meaning consumers chose to share their viewing data as part of the setup process for their television. This creates a sample rather than a census: only a portion of TV households in any given market are represented in Samba's footprint at any point in time.

Because the footprint is a sample, the reach and tune-in figures in a Samba TV report are not direct counts of households or viewers. They are statistical projections from the ACR sample to the full addressable population, using demographic weights and modeling to make the sample representative of the broader market. The accuracy of those projections depends on how the weighting is constructed and how closely the ACR sample reflects the viewing habits of households not in the network.

This distinction matters when reading a Samba report. A figure like '4.2 million households reached' is a modeled estimate, not a headcount. The confidence interval around that estimate depends on the size of the underlying ACR sample for that particular cut of data. National, full-campaign figures are generally based on large samples and are more stable. Figures sliced to a single network, a single market, or a narrow demographic are based on smaller samples and carry more uncertainty, even if they are presented in the report without explicit error margins.

A few signs this check applies to a campaign:
  • The team refers to Samba TV reach figures as if they were exact counts when presenting results to stakeholders
  • The campaign report is used to make decisions at a specific level (e.g., by individual network or DMA) without any acknowledgment that those cuts may be thinner-sample projections
  • No one on the team has reviewed Samba TV's methodology documentation or discussed the sample-based nature of the data with the Samba account team
You may want to confirm that anyone presenting or acting on Samba TV data understands that the figures are modeled projections from an opt-in ACR sample and that the certainty of those projections varies by how the data is sliced.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer using Samba TV to measure which networks or dayparts drove TV-exposed shoppers to a website or store, you may want to confirm the team understands that the TV exposure counts in the Samba report are modeled from the ACR sample, not a census, so conversion rate calculations built on those figures inherit the same uncertainty.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV to identify which TV audiences convert after seeing a CTV or linear campaign, you may want to confirm the team treats Samba's reach and exposure figures as modeled estimates when sizing the TV-exposed segment used in any downstream attribution model.

educate.learn_more_note: Samba TV's ACR methodology, device footprint size, and weighting approach are detailed in documentation available through the Samba TV account team and partner portal. Contact your Samba TV account representative for the current methodology whitepaper.

investigate.intro: Review whether the team has documented its understanding of Samba TV's sample-based methodology and whether that understanding is reflected in how results are presented.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV report for the campaign in question. Look for any footnote, methodology note, or confidence indicator on the reported figures. Note whether sample sizes or margins of error are disclosed anywhere in the report.
Ask the team whether they have read Samba TV's methodology documentation or received a methodology briefing from the Samba account team. Confirm whether that documentation is on file.
Review any presentation, summary, or stakeholder report built from Samba TV data. Check whether figures are described as modeled estimates or whether they are presented as exact counts.

Contact the Samba TV account team if the methodology is unclear. Ask specifically: how is the ACR sample projected to the full population, and what sample sizes underlie the specific report cuts the team is relying on?

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the team has reviewed Samba TV's methodology, whether the sample-based nature of the figures is reflected in how results are presented, and whether any sample size or confidence information is available in the report. A team with documented methodology review that qualifies reported figures as modeled estimates passes. A team that treats Samba TV figures as exact counts with no methodology review on file is a fail, recorded with the specific figures being mischaracterized.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the team has reviewed Samba TV's methodology documentation and how the reported figures are described in stakeholder presentations above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the team is treating Samba TV reach and tune-in figures as census-level counts rather than modeled projections from an opt-in Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) sample. Samba TV's measurement is built on a footprint of participating smart TVs, and the reported figures are statistical projections from that sample to the full population. Presenting those figures as exact counts, particularly at narrow report cuts such as individual networks or markets, overstates the precision of the data. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing Samba TV's methodology documentation with the account team, adding a qualifications note to any stakeholder report built on Samba data, and treating figures from narrow report cuts as estimates with meaningful uncertainty rather than definitive measurements.

audit_checks.title: Small-base report cuts are used as if the underlying ACR sample is large enough to support precise conclusions
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A figure that looks specific, such as reach within a single DMA or tune-in on a niche cable network, may rest on a small number of ACR devices that happened to record that content in that market. Projecting from a thin sample to a population produces a number with wide uncertainty, and acting on that number as if it were reliable can lead to budget or creative decisions that are not grounded in real signal.

educate.base:
When a Samba TV report is sliced to a narrow cut, such as a specific designated market area (DMA), a single cable network, a particular daypart, or a small demographic segment, the number of ACR devices contributing to that calculation shrinks. The projected figure still appears in the report as a specific number, but the statistical reliability of that projection depends on the sample size behind it. A small underlying sample produces a figure with a wide margin of error, even when no error range is displayed in the report.

This is a general property of sample-based measurement that applies across research and audience data methodologies. The Media Rating Council (MRC) guidelines for audience reach measurement address the principle that figures derived from small base counts require special handling and should not be treated with the same confidence as figures from large samples. Samba TV applies weighting to make its ACR sample more representative, but weighting cannot substitute for having enough devices in a given cell to produce a stable projection.

In practice, a campaign team may see a report showing, for example, that a particular DMA delivered a specific number of incremental household exposures on a specific network. If the ACR footprint in that DMA is thin, the figure is volatile: a small change in which devices reported data during that period could produce a substantially different number. Using that figure to decide whether to renew placement on that network or shift budget to another market treats noise as signal.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The team is using Samba TV data to compare performance across individual DMAs or individual networks and making placement decisions based on those comparisons without knowing the underlying sample sizes
  • The campaign report shows figures for very small segments, such as a narrow demographic on a niche network in a single market, and those figures are being cited as precise findings
  • No one on the team has asked Samba TV what sample sizes underlie the specific cuts they are acting on
You may want to ask the Samba TV account team which cuts of the report are considered statistically reliable for this campaign's geography and audience before using specific cuts from the report to guide decisions.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer using Samba TV to identify which regional markets showed the strongest TV-to-site correlation, you may want to confirm the underlying ACR sample sizes for each DMA before ranking markets by Samba-reported performance, as smaller markets may produce less stable figures.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser comparing network-level performance in Samba TV data, you may want to confirm which networks have large enough ACR samples in the campaign's target markets before concluding that a low-performing network should be cut.

learn_more.source_title: Minimum Standards for Media Rating Research

educate.learn_more_note: Samba TV provides guidance on which report cuts are considered statistically reliable for a given campaign through the account team and partner documentation. Contact your Samba TV account representative to request sample size information for the specific geographies, networks, and segments your campaign is using.

investigate.intro: Identify which report cuts the team is actively using to make decisions, and determine whether the underlying ACR samples for those cuts are large enough to support the conclusions being drawn.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV report and identify the most specific cuts the team is using: individual DMAs, individual networks, narrow demographic segments, or short dayparts.

Check whether the report includes any sample size indicators, base count disclosures, or reliability flags for those cuts. Note whether any figures are suppressed or flagged as low-base in the report interface.

Contact the Samba TV account team and ask specifically which of the cuts you are relying on have sample sizes considered reliable for the campaign's scope. Request any available guidance on which figures should be treated as directional rather than precise.

Review how the team is using each narrow-cut figure: is it being cited as a definitive finding, or is it being treated as a directional signal? Document the distinction for any figures that may rest on thin samples.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records which specific report cuts are being used to make decisions and whether the team has confirmed the underlying sample reliability for those cuts with Samba TV. Cuts where the team has confirmation of adequate sample size or treats the figures as directional pass. Cuts where precise conclusions are being drawn without any inquiry into the underlying sample are a fail, recorded with the specific cut and the decision being made from it.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the specific report cuts driving decisions and whether sample reliability has been confirmed with the Samba TV account team above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that narrow Samba TV report cuts, including specific designated market areas (DMAs) and individual networks, are being used to guide placement and budget decisions without confirmation that the underlying Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) sample sizes support those conclusions. Figures from narrow report cuts may rest on smaller ACR samples and carry more uncertainty than the presented numbers suggest. {{voice_recommend}} requesting sample size or base count guidance from the Samba TV account team for the specific cuts driving decisions, treating figures from thin-sample cuts as directional rather than definitive, and reserving precise comparative conclusions for cuts where sample adequacy has been confirmed.

audit_checks.title: Team has not reviewed how Samba TV weights and projects from the ACR sample to the full population
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without understanding how Samba projects from its ACR footprint to the population, the team cannot assess where the methodology is strongest, where it may introduce more uncertainty, or how to interpret discrepancies between Samba's figures and other measurement sources. The numbers are accepted or rejected without the context needed to evaluate them.

educate.base:
Samba TV constructs its audience projections by applying demographic and behavioral weights to the ACR data collected from its network of opt-in smart TVs. Weighting adjusts the raw sample to more closely match the composition of the full TV-viewing population in characteristics such as age, gender, household income, and geography. The projected figures in a Samba TV report reflect this weighted, modeled output rather than raw device counts.

The specifics of how Samba constructs its weights, which third-party census data it uses as a reference, how often it recalibrates those weights, and how it handles under-represented geographies or demographics are documented in Samba's methodology materials, which are generally available to partners and advertisers through the account team. Reviewing this methodology does not require technical expertise. It means understanding the broad structure: the sample is weighted to a reference population, the resulting figures are projections, and the precision of those projections varies by how well the sample reflects the specific segment being reported on.

Campaigns that run alongside other TV measurement sources, such as a Nielsen panel or a Comscore campaign rating, will often see different numbers between those sources and Samba TV. Some difference is expected because each uses a different measurement approach, a different sample, and different weighting methodology. Understanding Samba's projection approach gives the team context for evaluating whether a discrepancy between sources is within a reasonable range or signals a configuration issue worth investigating.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The team cannot describe, in general terms, how Samba projects from its ACR sample to the full population
  • When Samba's figures differ from another measurement source, the team has no basis for assessing whether the difference is expected or anomalous
  • No one has requested or reviewed Samba TV's methodology documentation since the measurement relationship began
You may want to schedule a methodology review with the Samba TV account team before the next campaign flight, so the team has a shared baseline for interpreting results.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running both a Samba TV measurement study and a separate attribution or panel measurement, you may want to confirm the team has reviewed Samba's projection methodology before comparing its figures to those from the other source, so any discrepancy is evaluated against a known methodological difference rather than treated as an unexplained conflict.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV reach data alongside first-party site traffic to calculate TV-exposed conversion rates, you may want to confirm the team understands how Samba's projected reach figures are constructed before using them as the denominator in a conversion rate calculation.

learn_more.source_title: Minimum Standards for Media Rating Research

educate.learn_more_note: Samba TV's full methodology documentation, including its weighting approach, reference population sources, and recalibration schedule, is available through the Samba TV account team and partner portal. Contact your Samba TV account representative to request the current methodology whitepaper.

investigate.intro: Determine whether the team has reviewed Samba TV's projection methodology and whether that understanding is available to anyone interpreting the campaign results.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Ask the team whether anyone has reviewed Samba TV's methodology documentation. If yes, confirm the documentation is on file and that the key points, specifically how the ACR sample is weighted and projected to the population, are understood.

If methodology documentation has not been reviewed, contact the Samba TV account team and request a methodology briefing or whitepaper. Ask specifically: what reference population is used for weighting, how often the weights are recalibrated, and how Samba handles geographies or demographics where the ACR sample is thinner.

If the campaign runs alongside another measurement source, compare the figures from each source for a shared metric such as total reach or impressions. Note the percentage difference and ask the Samba account team whether that difference falls within the expected range given the two methodologies.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the team has reviewed Samba TV's projection methodology, whether that documentation is on file, and whether the team can explain in general terms how ACR data is weighted and projected. A team with documented methodology review and a shared understanding of the projection approach passes. A team with no methodology review on file and no ability to explain the projection approach is a fail, recorded with a note on which decisions are being made without this context.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the team has reviewed Samba TV's projection methodology and whether that documentation is on file above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the team working with Samba TV data on {{site_name}} has not reviewed how Samba projects from its opt-in Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) sample to the full TV-viewing population. Without that context, the team cannot evaluate whether reported figures are reliable for the specific cuts it is acting on, or assess whether discrepancies between Samba's output and other measurement sources are within an expected range. {{voice_recommend}} requesting a methodology briefing from the Samba TV account team, securing the current methodology whitepaper for reference, and establishing a shared team understanding of the projection approach before using Samba data to guide placement or budget decisions.
